coredata.dc:title: Photoswitchable carbon-dot liposomes mediate catalytic cascade reactions for amplified dynamic treatment of tumor cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yu-Feng
Lin, Yu-Syuan
Huang, Tzu-Yun
Wei, Shih-Chun
Wu, Ren-Siang
Huang, Chih-Ching
Huang, Yu-Fen
Chang, Huan-Tsung
coredata.dc:description:
Most biochemical reactions that occur in living organisms are catalyzed by a series of enzymes and proceed in a tightly controlled manner. The development of artificial enzyme cascades that resemble multienzyme complexes in nature is of current interest due to their potential in various applications. In this study, a nanozyme based on photoswitchable carbon-dot liposomes (CDsomes) was developed for use in programmable catalytic cascade reactions. These CDsomes prepared from triolein are amphiphilic and self-assemble into liposome-like structures in an aqueous environment. CDsomes feature excitation-dependent photoluminescence and, notably, can undergo reversible switching between a fluorescent on-state and nonfluorescent off-state under different wavelengths of light irradiation. This switching ability enables the CDsomes to exert photocatalytic oxidase- and peroxidase-like activities in their on- (bright) and off- (dark) states, respectively, resulting in the conversion of oxygen molecules into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), followed by the generation of active hydroxyl radicals (
OH). The two steps of oxygen activation can be precisely controlled in a sequential manner by photoirradiation at different wavelengths. Catalytic reversibility also enables the CDsomes to produce sufficient reactive oxygen species (ROS) to effectively kill tumor cells. Our results reveal that CDsomes is a promising photo-cycling nanozyme for precise tumor phototherapy through regulated programmable cascade reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dot-liposomes
Photoswitching
Photocatalysts
Cascade reactions
Enzyme mimics
Photodynamic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of photocatalytic oxidase-mimic activity of CDsomes
Determination of peroxidase-mimic activity of CDsomes
Determination of band structure parameters of CDsomes by cyclic voltammetry
Photocatalytic oxidase- and peroxidase-mimic activities of CDsomes
Photocatalytic CDsomes for self-boosting ROS-based dynamic therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram representing the synthesis of CDsomes and (b) the corresponding TEM images. (c) Fluorescence microscopy images of CDsomes observe in (i) bright field, (ii) λEx = 365 nm/λEm = 420 nm, (iii) λEx = 475 nm/λEm = 520 nm, (iv) λEx = 530 nm/λEm = 575 nm. (d) Schematic diagram of photoswitching action of CDsomes after sequentially excited by 530 nm and 365 nm.

(a) Schematic diagram demonstrating the photocatalytic oxidase and peroxidase mimic activities of CDsomes triggered by light of different wavelengths. The catalytic performance of CDsomes (100 μg mL−1 in PBS) under different experimental conditions was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence of (b) resorufin (oxidized product of amplex red) using an amplex red/HRP assay and (c) hydroxyl terephthalic acid (TPAOH) using terephthalic acid (TPA) as an
OH probe. The CDsomes solution purged with N2 or incubated with H2O2 (200 μM) was irradiated with 365-nm light (50 mW cm−2, 5 min) or 530-nm light (200 mW cm−2, 10 min) or 365-nm followed by 530-nm light. The fluorescence readout of resorufin or TPAOH was linearly proportional to the quantity of H2O2 or
OH·H2O2 (20 μM) or
OH generated by mixing ferrous ions (1 mM) and H2O2 (200 μM) were used as the control groups. (d) CDsomes can promote the accumulation of
OH through repeated irradiation cycles of 365- and 530-nm light.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of CDsomes. The inset shows the Tauc plot for absorption edge determination. (b) Cyclic voltammogram of CDsomes (10 mg mL−2) in (i) positive scan and (ii) negative scan in 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate ([NBu4][BF4]) at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. (c) The possible mechanism by which CDsomes generate H2O2 by photocatalysis under 365-nm light irradiation. (d) The FTIR spectra of CDsomes (200 μg mL−1) detected in the dark or irradiated with 365-nm light (50 mW cm−2) or 530-nm light (200 mW cm−2).

(a) The EPR spectra of CDsomes (100 μg mL−1 in ultrapure H2O) detected in the dark or irradiated with 365-nm light (50 mW cm−2) or 530-nm light (200 mW cm−2) for 30 min. (b) Fluorescence intensity decay of CDsomes (100 μg mL−1 in ddH2O) under continuous irradiation of 530-nm light (50 mW cm−2) in the presence or absence of H2O2.

(a) Fluorescence microscopy image of Tramp-C1 cells after 10-min uptake of CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) observed in (i) bright field, (ii) λEx = 365 nm/λEm = 420 nm, (iii) λEx = 475 nm/λEm = 520 nm, (iv) λEx = 530 nm/λEm = 575 nm. (b) AlamarBlue cytotoxicity assay of Tramp-C1 cells incubated with CDsomes of various concentrations under different light treatments. After 10 min of CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) treatment, cells were subjected to 365-nm light (25 mW cm−2, 1 min) or 365-nm followed by 530-nm light (50 mW cm−2, 5 min). The cells were grown for another 24 h prior to the cytotoxicity measurement. (c) Flow cytometry histograms of the intracellular ROS level of Tramp-C1 cells treated with CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) under the above light irradiation conditions. Cells were stained with 2′-7′dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, 25 μM) for 30 min prior to CDsomes treatment and flow cytometry analysis. (d) Live and dead staining of Tramp-C1 cells treated with CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) under the above light irradiation conditions. Cells were stained with a Live/dead™ cell imaging kit for 30 min prior to fluorescence microscopy analysis. The white circle indicates the light exposure area. (e) Cytotoxicity to Tramp-C1 cells induced by CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) with multiple irradiation cycles. The experiment was repeated three times and results are expressed as mean ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Photoswitchable carbon-dot liposomes mediate catalytic cascade reactions for amplified dynamic treatment of tumor cells

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu 30013, Taiwan (Y.F. Huang). Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan (H.T. Chang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic oxidase- and peroxidase-mimic properties of carbon-dot liposomes (CDsomes) have been investigated for in situ cascade reactions. The programming of multienzyme-mimic cascades is attributed to the conspicuous photoswitchable property of CDsomes, which offers sufficient ROS and thus kill cancer cells with high spatiotemporal resolutions.

Most biochemical reactions that occur in living organisms are catalyzed by a series of enzymes and proceed in a tightly controlled manner. The development of artificial enzyme cascades that resemble multienzyme complexes in nature is of current interest due to their potential in various applications. In this study, a nanozyme based on photoswitchable carbon-dot liposomes (CDsomes) was developed for use in programmable catalytic cascade reactions. These CDsomes prepared from triolein are amphiphilic and self-assemble into liposome-like structures in an aqueous environment. CDsomes feature excitation-dependent photoluminescence and, notably, can undergo reversible switching between a fluorescent on-state and nonfluorescent off-state under different wavelengths of light irradiation. This switching ability enables the CDsomes to exert photocatalytic oxidase- and peroxidase-like activities in their on- (bright) and off- (dark) states, respectively, resulting in the conversion of oxygen molecules into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), followed by the generation of active hydroxyl radicals (
OH). The two steps of oxygen activation can be precisely controlled in a sequential manner by photoirradiation at different wavelengths. Catalytic reversibility also enables the CDsomes to produce sufficient reactive oxygen species (ROS) to effectively kill tumor cells. Our results reveal that CDsomes is a promising photo-cycling nanozyme for precise tumor phototherapy through regulated programmable cascade reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes are macromolecular biocatalysts and can promote various organic reactions that are vital in biological systems. Enzymes can also work together in sequence in multistep reactions, the so-called cascade reaction, in which the product of one enzyme becomes the substrate of another enzyme. In living cells, cascade reactions are often implemented in multienzyme complexes in a confined compartment (organelle) to improve the overall reaction efficiency. Peroxisomes are a representative example of a variety of enzymes that are delimited by a single membrane and can execute numerous metabolic reactions in a streamlined and timely manner, thereby promptly responding to environmental stimuli [1]. Inspired by this architecture, liposomes and polymersomes are attractive micro/nanocompartments for embedding or entrapping a set of natural/synthetic enzyme(s) for the development of artificial organelles, micro/nanoreactors, and therapeutic delivery carriers [2,3]. Through the modular design of core-building blocks and terminal capping moieties, external factors (e.g., pH, temperature, light, redox conditions, etc.) can be utilized to actuate programmed enzymatic reaction events [4–6]. Such design of enzymatic reactions holds great prospects in future biomedical applications, from self-regulating bioreactors to smart micro/nanomachines.

Nanozymes, a type of artificial enzymes, are catalytic nanomaterials designed to mimic the structures and functions of natural enzymes. A variety of nanomaterials, including metals, metal oxides and carbon-based nanoparticles (NPs), which exhibit tunable catalytic activities, have been discovered as promising mimetics of enzymes such as peroxidase, catalase, superoxide dismutase, and oxidase [7]. Nanozymes also offer the advantages of facile production, low cost, excellent storage stability, reusability and robustness under harsh conditions, making them as good alternatives to natural enzymes for analytical, environmental and biomedical applications [8,9]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials, have emerged as an important metal-free catalyst by virtue of their efficient electron transfer and light-harvesting capabilities [8,10,11]. For example, Shi et al. first discovered that CDs prepared from candle soot possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity [12]. In addition, heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen, boron, sulfur, and phosphorus have also been introduced into carbon nanomaterials, giving them additional enzyme-mimicking activities or enhancing their intrinsic enzyme properties [10,11,13,14]. According to the report by Fan et al., N-doped porous carbon nanospheres can exhibit four enzyme-like properties and can regulate the production and removal of ROS [15]. Collectively, the development of carbon-based nanomaterials that can integrate different catalytic functions in a single system represents a significant advance in multienzyme cascade/tandem reactions.

CDs possess an extraordinary ability to capture broadband incident light, which also makes them particularly promising in light-driven photocatalysis [11,14,16,17]. For example, magnesium-nitrogen-embedded CDs derived from the extract of plant leaves have been successfully utilized for the sunlight-induced photocatalytic degradation of pollutant dyes due to the in situ generation of highly reactive electron–hole pairs and the subsequent ROS [18]. In addition, Li et al. also reported that CDs can function as an effective near infrared light-driven photocatalyst for the selective oxidation of benzyl alcohol in the presence of H2O2 as an oxidant [19]. In general, optical and electronic tunability and efficient photoinduced electron transfer capabilities make CDs an appealing sensitized photocatalyst that can act alone or in combination with other catalysts to actuate numerous photocatalytic reactions [16,17]. The design of nanozymes with photoresponsiveness is also promising for enabling precise control of one-pot multistep catalytic reactions in a cascade manner.

Previously, we reported the successful development of lipid-like CDs from triolein through a simple heating and hydrolysis process (Fig. 1 a) [20,21]. These CDs are amphiphilic in nature and can self-assemble into uniform unilamellar vesicles (CDsomes) in aqueous solution (Fig. 1b). Similar to most reported CDs, CDsomes display excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) in the blue to red-light regions (Fig. 1c and Figure S1 ). More interestingly, these fluorescent nanoprobes are photostable and photoswitchable. They can be reversibly switched between a fluorescent on-state and nonfluorescent off-state under photoirradiation over the wavelength regions of 365–420 nm and 510–540 nm, respectively [20,21]. The corresponding fluorescence changes of CDsomes were recorded by light irradiation at wavelength of 365 and 530 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d). In this study, we explored the corresponding catalytic activity and found that CDsomes can mimic photocatalytic oxidase and peroxidase in the on- (bright) and off- (dark) state, respectively. Under light irradiation at 365 nm, CDsomes in the on-state can accelerate the conversion of oxygen molecules into H2O2, which in turn can generate active
OH when switched to the off-state by 530 nm irradiation. As a result, oxygen activation can be achieved through a programmable catalytic cascade, which is precisely controlled by photoirradiation at different wavelengths. The multistep reactions mediated by this novel class of CDsomes enable sufficient generation of ROS and thus effectively kill tumor cells. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the prominent photoswitchable property of CDs has been explored in the programming of multienzyme-mimic cascade reactions. The vesicular self-assembly architecture built by lipid-like CDs also ensures a finely tailored spatial and timely configuration, thereby greatly enhancing the overall activity.

Sodium phosphate dibasic (>98 %), sodium phosphate monobasic potassium chloride (>99 %), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (>99 %), sodium chloride (>99 %), sodium hydroxide (>99 %), amplex red (>98 %), terephthalate (TPA, >98 %), 5, 5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, >97 %) and 2′-7′dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA, >97 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Triolein (>98 %) was obtained from Chemlin (Nanjing, Gulou District, China). Methanol (>99.5 %), ethanol (>99.5 %), and ethyl ester (>99.5 %) were purchased from J.T.Baker (Center Valley, PA, United States). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were obtained from Gibco (Grand Island, NY, United States). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) was purchased from TCI (Tokyo chemical industry, co., ltd.) Penicillin–streptomycin, Trypsin-EDTA, Live/dead™ cell imaging kit, and AlamarBlue were bought from Invitrogen (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, United States). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q system of Millipore (Billerica, MA, USA) was used in all experiments.

CDsomes were prepared through a dry heating and process. In brief, 30.0 g triolein was placed in a furnace of Nabertherm GmbH (Bahnhofstr, Lilienthal, Germany) at 220 °C for 3 days under ambient pressure. During the heating process, triolein slowly decomposed into oleic acid, glyceride and further polymerized to form a jelly-like structure. Afterwards, the dry heating product (5.0 g) was treated with NaOH solution (0.20 M) and then purified through dialysis (regenerated cellulose, molecular weight cut off = 15 kDa, Spectrum) against ultrapure water. The purified product was lyophilized and the calculated yield was 68 % (w/w). After resuspending the lyophilized product in aqueous solution, self-assembled CDsomes (i.e., CDs-constructed liposome-like structures) were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of photocatalytic oxidase-mimic activity of CDsomes
ce:section.ce:para: The CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) were freshly prepared in PBS. The subsequent photocatalysis experiments followed the respect recipes: kept in dark, exposed to 365-nm light (50 mW cm−2, 5 min), 530-nm light (200 mW cm−2, 10 min) or 365-nm light followed by 530-nm light. To probe H2O2 produced after light stimuli, amplex red and HRP with concentrations of 20 μM and 0.1 U, respectively, were added to the solution. The fluorescence of the oxidized product, resorufin, was measured by fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 550 nm. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at emission wavelength of 590 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of peroxidase-mimic activity of CDsomes
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the peroxidase-mimic performance of CDsomes, TPA was used as an effective probe for the detection of
OH. TPA (1.0 mM) was added to the PBS solution containing 100 μg mL−1 CDsomes, and then subjected to the different light irradiation mentioned above. The hydroxylated product TPAOH was measured by fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at emission wavelength of 425 nm.

EPR spectroscopy was used to conform the generation of H2O2 and
OH by using DMPO as a spin trap. In brief, 100 μL CDsomes (100 μg mL−1) was mixed with 100 μL of DMPO in a dark Eppendorf tube. The subsequent photocatalysis experiments followed the respect recipes: kept in dark, exposed to 365-nm light (50 mW cm−2, 5 min), 530-nm light (200 mW cm−2, 10 min) or 365-nm followed by 530-nm light. To further confirm H2O2 production, catalase with 1000 U mL−1 was added into the CDsomes solutions prior to the light exposure experiments. To detect the intermediate radicals generated by CDsomes after light exposure, no spin trap is required. EPR spectrometer parameters were as follows: g–factor, 2.00627; modulation frequency, 100 kHz; modulation amplitude, 0.1 mT; microwave frequency, 9.8 GHz; microwave power, 15 mW; scan range, 100 G; time constant, 0.328 s; receiver gain, 30; sampling time, 20 ms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of band structure parameters of CDsomes by cyclic voltammetry
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the band structure of CDsomes, the electrochemical cyclic voltammetry was performed at room temperature using CHI 760D electrochemical workstation. In this three-electrode configuration, silver/silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) and platinum wire (diameter 1 mm) were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. CDsomes (10.0 mg mL−1) were prepared in dimethyl sulfoxide containing 0.10 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate ([NBu4][BF6]) as the supporting electrolyte. The solution was stirred and degassed by bubbling nitrogen for a few minutes prior to each measurement. The scan rate was 10 mV s−1.

Murine prostate cancer cell line (Tramp-C1) and embryonic mouse fibroblast cell line (NIH-3T3) were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). The Tramp-C1 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin and maintained at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 in a humidified incubator. The cells were passaged whenever the cells reached 80 % confluence. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDsomes with different light irradiation conditions, Tramp-C1 cells or NIH-3T3 cells (1.0 × 105) were seeded into 96 well-plates and cultured for 12 h. 100 μL of CDsomes with a series of concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg mL−1 in PBS) were applied to the cells. Afterwards, treated cells were subjected to 365-nm light (25 mW cm−2, 1 min), 530-nm light (50 mW cm−2, 5 min) or 365-nm light followed by 530-nm light, respectively. CDsomes were incubated with the cells for another 1 h, then 100 μL of fresh growth medium (2×) was added and grown for another 24 h. The cell viability was measured by using AlamarBlue assay.

Intracellular ROS production was detected using the fluorescence dye DCFH-DA. Tramp-C1 cells (4.0 × 105) were seeded into 48 well-plates for 12 h adhesion. The probe DCFH-DA (25 μM) was incubated with the cell culture for 30 min. After removing the excess DCFH-DA, 100 μL of CDsomes (100 μg mL−1 in PBS) were added, and the cells were further subjected to different light treatments. The cells were trypsinized and analyzed for DCF fluorescence by flow cytometry (BD Biosciences FACSCalibur).

To perform spatially specific cell damage via sequential light irradiation in a confined area, Live/deadTM cell imaging kit (Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA, United States) was applied to visualize the cell damage under light exposure in the presence of CDsomes. The cells were plated on the 3.5 cm culture dish with a density of 80 % conference. CDsomes with 100 μg mL−1 were added for 30 min incubation then wash twice by 1 × PBS to remove the excess CDsomes. Before light treatment, 2 μM of calcein-AM and 4 μM PI were added to the cells. The light exposure periods of 365 nm and 530 nm were 3 and 5 min, respectively. Fluorescence images of live/dead staining were taken at 30 min after respective light exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic oxidase- and peroxidase-mimic activities of CDsomes
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic behavior of CDsomes in the on-state or off-state under different light exposure was investigated (Fig. 2 a). Amplex red, which can produce the red fluorescent oxidation product (resorufin), was used in combination with HRP to detect the generation of H2O2 (the dotted line in Fig. 2b). As depicted in Fig. 2b, CDsomes can yield highly fluorescent resorufin under 365-nm light irradiation, while a negligible fluorescence signal can be detected under 530-nm light irradiation. The fluorescence signal also decreases as the dissolved oxygen decreases after purging with nitrogen. This finding indicates that CDsomes exhibit photocatalytic oxidase mimetic activity in the on state when triggered by UV light. >10 μM H2O2 was formed after 5 min of irradiation. Additionally, TPA, a known probe for
OH (the dotted line in Fig. 2c), was used to characterize the peroxidase-imitating behavior. In the presence of H2O2, the generation of the fluorescent product TPAOH (hydroxyl terephthalic acid) was detected as the CDsomes were switched to the off state by 530-nm light (Fig. 2c). In contrast, only a slight change in fluorescence was observed when CDsomes were kept in the on state ( Figure S2 ). This result suggests that CDsomes can catalyze the conversion of H2O2 into
OH in response to green light exposure. Compared with stimulation by green light alone, sequential irradiation of UV and green light can lead to the evolution of
OH, further indicating the successful activation of multienzyme-mimic reactions in one pot by light irradiation. The EPR spectra recorded in situ also confirm the corresponding catalytic reactions ( Figure S3 ). CDsomes under UV light irradiation provide a typical quartet signal with a 1:2:2:1 intensity ratio corresponding to the DMPO–OH adduct [22]. The signal disappeared after the addition of H2O2 scavenger catalase, indicating that H2O2 was produced. After subsequent irradiation with green light, the catalytic conversion of H2O2 to
OH was promoted. Most importantly, when CDsomes were exposed to a series of UV (365 nm) and green (530 nm) light, the biomimetic cascade enzyme reaction can proceed repeatedly. As depicted in Fig. 2d, excessive ROS accumulates continuously with the sequential catalytic process controlled by light stimulation at varying wavelengths.

As mentioned above, CDs that can undergo broadband light absorption, fast charge carrier migration and efficient surface redox reactions have emerged as a popular material for modern photocatalyst design [13,23–25]. Through the absorption of incident photons with energy equal to or exceeding the bandgap, an excited electron/hole pair is produced. The consequential photocatalytic redox reaction can take place as the photogenerated electrons and holes successfully migrate to the catalyst surface [16,17,26]. In more detail, Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the CDsomes. A fundamental absorption band is centered in the UV region, and the absorption edge is at approximately 408 nm [27]. The corresponding optical band gap (Eg) is estimated to be 3.04 eV according to the classical Tauc approach (inset in Fig. 3a) [27]. Light with a wavelength below 408 nm is expected to provide sufficient momentum to enable the transitions. Moreover, the electrochemical behaviors of CDsomes were studied by cyclic voltammetry [28]. The current-voltage curves with respect to the positive and negative potential regions are displayed in Fig. 3b. Each observed process involves several irreducible waves, indicating that different electron transfers occur in the defined surface moieties present in CDsomes. Our results are in agreement with those of previous studies [29,30], demonstrating that the versatile but complex photoredox or redox capabilities of CDs are associated with the heterogeneous active sites generated during the synthesis. Based on the onset potentials of the waves, the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) are estimated to be approximately −3.13 and −6.12 eV, respectively, according to the following equations [31]:
(1) EHOMO (eV) = − (Eonset, ox vs Ag/AgCl + 0.197 + 4.74).
(2) ELUMO (eV) = − (Eonset, red vs Ag/AgCl + 0.197 + 4.74).

The energy difference is appropriately equal to the optical band gap extrapolated from the UV–Vis spectra. The energy value obtained can also be approximated as the potential oxidation or reduction strength of CDsomes to achieve possible redox reactions [30]. As H2O2 is the main active oxidation species detected in the photocatalytic process, it is highly likely that the photoexcited CDsomes have sufficiently negative potentials to drive the reduction of molecular oxygen to H2O2 (Fig. 3c). In more detail, Fig. 3d shows the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of the CDsomes with different light illumination. The peak intensities associated with endoperoxide and chemisorbed molecular oxygen at 842/885 (νCOO), 1103 (νCO), and 957 cm−1 (νOO) [32,33] increased after 365-nm light irradiation, respectively. This result is consistent with previous findings on photocatalytic H2O2 generation [34,35], suggesting that O2 can be reduced by electrons photogenerated from CDsomes to form intermediate endoperoxide moieties across the aromatic ring domains, leading to the subsequent release of H2O2. Due to the efficient formation of endoperoxide species on the photoexcited CDsomes triggered by 365-nm light, one-electron reduction of O2 will be significantly suppressed [34,35]. The absence of superoxide anion (
O2 −) in previous EPR tests ( Figure S3 ) also suggests that two-electron reduction may be beneficial. On the other hand, light with a wavelength above 408 nm is insufficient for electron and hole separation as mentioned earlier. Negligible changes in the FTIR signal are expected after illuminating CDsomes with 530-nm light (Fig. 3d).

Two electron reduction:
(3) O2 + 2e− + 2H+ → H2O2.

One electron reduction:
(4) O2 + e− →
O2 −.
(5)
O2 − + e− + 2H+ → H2O2

Additionally, according to previous studies, the on-off state of CD fluorescence is believed to be related to the change in redox status [20,36]. Therefore, EPR spectroscopy was used to study the respective state characteristics of the CDsomes. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the CDsomes showed a strong EPR signal at g = 1.998 during 530 nm light irradiation, while the EPR signal detected in the absence or presence of 365-nm light was less obvious. This result indicates the presence of long-lived intermediate radicals residing in the off state. In addition, the triplet state, which possesses a longer lifetime than the ordinary excited state, is considered to be the origin of the electron transfer process, leading to the generation of metastable radical states [37,38]. The postulated energy diagram of the off-state formation is highlighted in Figure S4 . It should also be pointed out that although conjugated double bonds, surface defects and abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs may be involved in the generation and stabilization of radicals [39,40], the exact molecular mechanism behind these processes remains to be explored. Moreover, similar to the other electron donor/acceptor molecules reported previously [29,41], H2O2 has been found to regulate the on-off fluorescence of CDsomes. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence reduction of CDsomes in response to green light was delayed by H2O2 in a dose-dependent manner. It was hypothesized that H2O2 can act as an electron acceptor to rescue CDsomes trapped in the off states ( Figure S4 ). As the radical state switches back to the ground state, H2O2 can also be simultaneously converted to
OH and OH–, respectively. The photoswitching cycles of CDsomes continue under green light irradiation until all H2O2 are consumed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic CDsomes for self-boosting ROS-based dynamic therapy
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the adaptability to biological systems, the tolerance of the photocatalytic cascade reaction of CDsomes was carefully checked. The activities of both H2O2 and OH radical generation changed separately less than 15 % and 20 % in the biological pH region from 5.0 to 9.0 ( Figure S5
a). The slight increase in peroxidase activity might have come from the increased reactivity of the OH radical toward TPA in an alkaline environment [42]. In the presence of 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), the OH radical generation activity reduced by only 15 % ( Figure S5
b) when the H2O2 generation activity did not significantly change. The slightly decreased activity might be due to the spontaneous chelating of
OH with serum proteins [43]. Compared with metallic catalysts which can be strongly deactivated through ligand binding of circulation albumin [44,45], we reason that CDsomes can be great candidates for biological applications. Inspired by the therapeutic potential of ROS in cancer treatment [46,47], the impact of CDsome-induced in situ photocatalytic cascade reactions on anticancer therapy was studied. Cellular uptake was first assessed by tracking the fluorescence of CDsomes incubated with mouse Tramp-C1 prostate cancer cells (Fig. 5 a) and mouse NIH-3T3 embryo fibroblast cells ( Figure S6
a). Observations of multicolor emission in the two cells at different excitation wavelengths indicate that CDsomes through their amphiphilic lipid properties can be readily internalized by different cell types. To evaluate the intracellular tumoricidal capacity of CDsomes-mediated photodynamic action, a standard AlamarBlue assay was performed. As shown in Fig. 5b and Figure S6
b, the dark toxicity of CDsomes up to 200 μg mL−1 is almost negligible to the two cell lines. This result shows adequate biocompatibility of the designed enzyme mimetics. However, exposing the treated cells to 365-nm light caused a significant decrease in cell viability. The therapeutic effect was further enhanced by 530-nm light irradiation. The photoactivated CDsomes led to remarkably less cell death in NIH-3 T3 cells than in Tramp-C1 cells. This result is consistent with previous findings, indicating that cancer cells are more susceptible to further ROS insults due to higher endogenous oxidative stress than normal cells.Panieri and Santoro [48]. To evaluate the ROS production involved in CDsomes-mediated phototoxicity, DCFH-DA staining was used to detect Tramp-C1 cells after treatment. As depicted in Fig. 5c, after exposure to UV light alone and in combination with green light, the DCF fluorescence generated by ROS increased by approximately 1.5- and 3-fold compared with the dark control, respectively. This finding agrees with the previous MTT results (Fig. 5b), revealing a strong correlation between ROS levels and the toxic effects triggered by the photoactivation of CDsomes. In addition, Tramp-C1 cells cotreated with catalase (H2O2 scavenger) exhibited a marked decrease in DCF fluorescence intensity ( Figure S7
a), whereas the signal was apparently proportional to the H2O2 input ( Figure S7
b). This result reveals that the production and conversion of H2O2 is involved in the light-initiated biocatalytic cascade reaction. It is also worth noting that ROS generation in the next peroxidase-mimicking cycle was more pronounced when H2O2 was self-supplied by UV exposure, even compared with a large amount (100 μM) of exogenous source input. The CDsomes developed in this study are effective all-in-one carriers that can integrate a number of photocatalytic reactions in the cascade sequence. The fluorescent reaction products with respect to H2O2 and
OH can also be successfully detected under conditions mimicking the cell culture media, again illustrating that CDsomes can perform photocatalysis in complex biological systems ( Figure S5
b). Confocal microscopy was further used to visualize CDsomes-mediated cell damage during photoirradiation (Fig. 5d). The cells were stained with a Live/dead™ cell imaging kit for viability assessment. According to Fig. 5d, damaged cells identified by red-fluorescent propidium iodide (PI) staining appeared in the light-illuminated area, while cells outside this area remained viable (green fluorescence). Additionally, the brightness of the PI signal becomes more evident when green light is added after UV irradiation. These results show that CDsomes combined with external light sources enable precise control of photocatalytic reactions at high spatiotemporal resolutions. Upon repeated exposure to UV and green light, the photoinduced cell damage continuously increased over irradiation cycles (Fig. 5e). It is believed that this remote-controlled artificial nanozyme with unique optically switchable properties could greatly impact future material applications.

In summary, CDsomes with optical switching properties has been discovered as promising enzyme-mimetic materials exhibiting tunable catalytic activity for programmable cascade reactions. In the on- (bright) state, CDsomes can act as photocatalytic oxidase to convert oxygen molecules into H2O2 under 365-nm light irradiation. Switching of CDsomes to the off- (dark) state can be triggered by 530-nm light irradiation; the consequent peroxidase activity can generate active
OH using H2O2 as a fuel. Compared with conventional photocatalysts such as graphene oxide [49] g-C3N4 [50], TiO2 [51], and ZnO [52] reported so far, this study is the first attempt to exploit the photoswitchable property of CDs to manipulate the catalytic cascade reactions by irradiation with different wavelengths of light. Benefiting from the optical switching and catalytic reversibility, as well as the self-assembly vesicular architecture of the CDsomes, the sequential reactions can be well-tailored in a spatial and timely manner, thereby improving the overall catalytic efficiency. CDsomes are able to promote the accumulation of
OH through repeated irradiation cycles of 365- and 530-nm light, resulting in amplified dynamic therapy of tumor cells. Furthermore, because the CDsomes have the capacity to carry a wide variety of active ingredients (unpublished result), more complex multistep catalytic reactions can be programmed in a one-pot manner, providing the potential to unlock new avenues of biomedical applications.

This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan, under the contracts 108-2638-M-002-001-MY2, 110-2622-M-002-001, 110-2113-M-002-005-MY3, 110-2113-M-007-004, and 111-2113-M-007 -029.

Yu-Feng Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Yu-Syuan Lin: Methodology. Tzu-Yun Huang: Methodology. Shih-Chun Wei: Methodology. Ren-Siang Wu: Methodology. Chih-Ching Huang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yu-Fen Huang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Huan-Tsung Chang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Instrumentation Center at National Tsing Hua University (NTHU) for providing the EPR analysis. The assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) for TEM measurement is appreciated.
